food_subgroup: Noodle dish with beef, vegetables, and fish sauce dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese beef vermicelli bowl. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Noodle dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Vietnamese dry vermicelli with sauteed beef.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of dry rice vermicelli topped with sauteed beef and white onion, accompanied by fresh vegetables, herbs, pickled daikon and carrot, sweet-and-sour fish sauce dressing, and a crispy spring roll.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Bo Xao (Stir-fried Beef with Rice Vermicelli) — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked rice vermicelli, stir-fried beef, onion, vegetables, and fish sauce dressing
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Vietnamese dish as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for: cooked rice vermicelli (FDC 168871), stir-fried beef lean (FDC 174032), raw white onion (FDC 170000), mixed salad vegetables, fish sauce (FDC 173610), and sugar. Approximate component proportions per 100 g of assembled dish: rice vermicelli 35 g, beef 25 g, onion 10 g, vegetables/herbs 15 g, fish sauce dressing 10 g, spring roll 5 g. Values are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese noodle dishes.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI (boiled): GI = 53 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice vermicelli (white rice noodles). The assembled dish contains additional protein (beef) and fat (spring roll, dressing) which further attenuate the glycemic response; GI of 53 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate for the complete dish. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 450 g serving) / 100 = 44.3 g. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce dressing and spring roll
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or request reduced fish sauce dressing, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1890 mg per serving) substantially exceeds the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold for sodium-restricted diets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli base, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the rice vermicelli base contributes a glycemic load of approximately 44 g per serving; portion control is advised, and pairing with the protein-rich beef component helps moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and spring roll, combined with high sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the combined burden of approximately 1.4 g saturated fat per 100 g and high sodium (~420 mg/100 g); requesting reduced dressing and limiting the crispy spring roll component would improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún Bò Xào Hành Tây is a Vietnamese dry vermicelli bowl dish originating from Saigon (Ho Chi Minh City), now popular in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant scene. The dish consists of thin white rice vermicelli noodles served dry (not in broth), topped with wok-sauteed sliced beef and white onion that have been seasoned with garlic, fish sauce, and black pepper. The bowl is accompanied by a bed of fresh mixed vegetables and aromatic herbs (typically bean sprouts, lettuce, mint, and perilla), pickled daikon and carrot, and a crispy fried spring roll. The dish is finished with a sweet-and-sour fish sauce dressing (nước chấm) made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The beef is tender and savoury with a slight caramelised edge from high-heat stir-frying; the vermicelli is light and slightly chewy; the overall flavour profile is a balance of savoury, sweet, sour, and fresh herbal notes. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with 9.2 g protein, 4.1 g fat, and 18.5 g carbohydrate. The rice vermicelli base has a low glycemic index (GI ~53), though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~44 g). Sodium content is high due to the fish sauce dressing (~420 mg/100 g), making portion control of the dressing important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The beef component contributes iron, zinc, and vitamin B12. This dish is dairy-free but contains fish allergens and gluten from the spring roll wrapper.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite Vietnamese dish as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice vermicelli as the closest approved analogous food. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based estimation methodology is systematic and cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; actual restaurant serving values may vary depending on portion sizes, dressing quantity, and cooking method. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 3. Sodium content is likely to vary significantly depending on the quantity of fish sauce dressing applied; the estimate of 420 mg/100 g assumes a standard dressing application. 4. The dish is listed under 'Meat and alternatives' food group consistent with the input record, reflecting the beef as the primary protein component. 5. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Co Chung Restaurant) is not confirmed halal-certified; consumers should verify directly. 6. GI of 53 (low) is a conservative upper-bound estimate for the assembled dish; the presence of protein and fat from beef and spring roll would further attenuate the glycemic response in practice. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry moderate uncertainty. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component sources used for this dish estimation.
